data.title: Why Quobly, STMicro, Soitec See Quantum as a Manufacturing Challenge
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Quantum computing has no shortage of impressive laboratory results. What it still lacks, almost universally, is a credible path from those results to machines that can be manufactured, replicated, and deployed at scale. For Grenoble, France-based startup Quobly, that gap—rather than qubit physics itself—is the core problem to solve.

The company’s strategy is shaped by a deliberately unglamorous premise: If quantum processors are ever to move beyond bespoke demonstrators, they must be designed from the outset to live inside the constraints of the semiconductor industry. That conviction underpins the company’s reliance on fully depleted silicon-on-insulator (FD-SOI) technology and its close collaboration with STMicroelectronics (STMicro) and substrate supplier Soitec.

Rather than inventing an exotic qubit platform and worrying about scale later, Quobly is doing the opposite: starting with an industrial platform and accepting the constraints it imposes on qubit design.

From transistors to a scalable quantum architecture

The technical roots of Quobly’s approach go back to CEA-Leti and CNRS research showing that a standard FD-SOI transistor could host a spin qubit, with quantum information encoded in the spin of a single electron. What initially looked like a clever reuse of CMOS technology became a strategic insight.

FD-SOI’s buried oxide and back-gate control allow electrons to be positioned away from noisy interfaces, improving coherence and readout fidelity. Just as importantly, the platform allows quantum devices and classical transistors to coexist on the same die—an architectural choice that directly addresses one of quantum computing’s most stubborn bottlenecks.

“The goal is not to make a good qubit,” Nicolas Daval, Quobly’s chief engineering officer, told EE Times. “The goal is to make a system that will have millions of qubits and can solve real problems.” That framing is deliberate. In Daval’s view, quantum computing will not be limited by how well a single qubit performs under ideal conditions, but by how many qubits can be manufactured with predictable behavior.

At small scales, quantum noise dominates device performance. But once systems grow into the hundreds or thousands of qubits, process variability becomes just as dangerous as decoherence “When we say we need millions of qubits, what we really need is stability, reproducibility, and yield,” Daval said. “Those are things you only get from industrial fabs.”

This is where STMicro becomes central to Quobly’s strategy. By developing its devices inside STMicro’s 300-mm FD-SOI manufacturing environment, Quobly gains access to statistical process control, characterization infrastructure, and manufacturing cadence that would be impossible to recreate independently.

Speaking in December 2025 at a press event at Quobly’s headquarters in Grenoble, Philippe Magarshack, STMicro’s group VP for strategy, technology, and system architecture, emphasized that quantum computing must ultimately be treated as a manufacturing problem. “Building a quantum computer is not only a scientific question,” he said. “It is also a question of producing quantum computers at large scale and at an affordable cost.”

STMicro’s advantage, he argued, lies in developing new technologies on the same tools used for volume production. “Once the recipes are optimized, scaling becomes largely a matter of automation,” he said, pointing out that most of the process steps used for Quobly’s devices already exist in STMicro’s FD-SOI flows in their 300-mm manufacturing fabs near Grenoble.

In an industry accustomed to equating progress with smaller nodes, Quobly’s decision to anchor its roadmap on 28-nm FD-SOI may seem conservative. Daval argues the opposite: “Quantum devices are not transistors. What matters is the distance between qubits, not the gate length.”

In Quobly’s architecture, an inter-qubit spacing of around 100 nm strikes a balance between controllable interaction and electrical isolation. That geometry is readily achievable with mature 193-nm lithography, avoiding the cost and fragility of more advanced nodes. The choice also keeps Quobly aligned with existing FD-SOI ecosystems used for microcontrollers and low-power logic—an alignment that simplifies everything from yield learning to long-term cost models.

Inside Quobly’s labs, this industrial mindset is already visible. Device characterization follows workflows that resemble semiconductor process development more than academic experimentation. Thousands of structures per wafer are measured at room temperature to assess uniformity and variability before selected devices undergo cryogenic testing. The emphasis is on distributions and correlations, not on extracting peak performance from isolated samples. “We need statistical characterization,” Daval said. “Six-sigma access, the same tools the semiconductor industry uses for billions of transistors—we reuse all of that.”

Weekly review loops with STMicro allow deviations to be traced back to specific process steps, enabling incremental refinement without halting fabrication. The goal is continuous learning rather than episodic breakthroughs.

The right substrate lays the foundation for an industrial European bet

The substrate plays an outsized role in silicon spin qubits, where magnetic noise from nuclear spins directly impacts coherence. Natural silicon contains multiple isotopes, including silicon-29, whose nuclear spin degrades qubit performance.

“For quantum applications, the isotope of silicon is the key parameter,” Christophe Maleville, Soitec’s chief technology officer, told EE Times. “We specifically use silicon-28 to make sure the interaction of the device happens in a nuclear-spin-free environment.”

The challenge for Soitec was not isotopic purification alone but delivering that purity in wafers fully compatible with industrial FD-SOI production. According to Maleville, today’s quantum-ready substrates meet the same thickness and uniformity specifications as classical FD-SOI—on the order of ±5 angstroms for the silicon layer. “That uniformity makes it easier to confine electrons and to use back-biasing as a second gate to fine-tune qubit interaction,” he said.

The approach does not suffer from wiring complexity, one of the most visible scaling barriers in quantum systems. Architectures that rely on individual external connections to each qubit quickly encounter physical and thermal limits inside cryostats. “At very small scale, you can wire everything externally,” Daval said. “But already at 100 qubits, wiring becomes a serious limitation. Co-integration is absolutely essential.”

Quobly’s architecture integrates classical control electronics directly alongside qubits, reducing external connections and enabling faster local feedback. The company’s Q100 milestone—a 100-physical-qubit device with on-chip control expected in 2027—is designed to demonstrate that this approach scales cleanly.

Magarshack reinforced this point at the event, noting that placing qubits and correction logic microns apart fundamentally changes system efficiency and error-correction latency.

Quobly’s roadmap is unapologetically centered on fault tolerance. Surface-code error correction demands large numbers of physical qubits but aligns naturally with two-dimensional solid-state layouts. “That’s not a problem for us,” Daval said. “We know how to produce a lot of qubits, and we know how to control them.”

Early systems will target research users and cloud access, with fault-tolerant machines aimed at chemistry, materials science, optimization, and cryptography later in the decade. Beyond the technical details, the Quobly–STMicro–Soitec collaboration reflects a distinctly European strategy—leveraging existing industrial strengths rather than attempting to outspend hyperscalers.

Instead of bespoke fabs or fragile vertical stacks, the partners are extending a proven semiconductor model into the quantum domain. As Maleville put it, “Staying close to mainstream process flows is essential for speed and industrialization.”

Whether this bet will ultimately outperform more exotic architectures remains uncertain. What is clear is that Quobly is reframing the quantum race around manufacturability and cost—areas where semiconductor discipline, not laboratory heroics, will decide the winners.

data.title: XConn Buy Unveils Marvell’s AI Connectivity Imperative
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Nearly a month after announcing its acquisition of optical interconnect startup Celestial AI, Marvell Technology has inked a $540 million deal to buy XConn Technologies, a developer of Compute Express Link (CXL) and Peripheral Component Interconnect Express (PCIe) switching silicon.

Together, these two deals telegraph Marvell’s ambition to become a connectivity powerhouse for both electrical and optical networking fabrics in AI and data center environments. These acquisitions also herald the battle for next-generation interconnect technologies in AI data center connectivity.

XConn holds a unique position in the interconnect market with its first hybrid switch, which supports both CXL and PCIe on a single chip. According to IDC analyst Taranvir Singh, access to this hybrid switching silicon for interconnects will improve Marvell’s position in the data center connectivity stack.

XConn, founded in 2020, launched the industry’s first CXL 2.0 and PCIe Gen 5.0 switch chip in 2024. Apollo provides 256 lanes with a total switching capacity of 2,048 Gbps. Next year, its Apollo 2 hybrid switch will integrate both CXL 3.1 and PCIe Gen 6.2 on a single chip. Here, switching refers to the process of data transfer across the data center.

Apollo integrates silicon for CXL and PCIe on a single chip. (Source: XConn)

A closer look at this deal reveals Marvell’s quest for two design building blocks in AI-driven data center connectivity: CXL and UALink. Let’s first look at CXL and its ability to facilitate bandwidth across multiple racks and thus address AI’s infamous memory wall.

Why CXL matters in data center connectivity

The purchase of XConn Technologies is Marvell’s largest acquisition in the CXL switching silicon space. In 2022, the company acquired privately held Tanzanite Silicon Solutions, a developer of CXL technology specializing in memory expansion, tiered memory, and memory pooling. These two acquisitions over the span of three years are a testament to Marvell’s strategic focus on memory-focused CXL technology that promises to overcome memory bottlenecks limiting AI accelerator performance.

CXL 3.1 switch for AI systems facilitates memory pooling, sharing, and expansion. (Source: XConn)

CXL—a technology developed by Intel to improve performance and remove bottlenecks between CPUs and other components like GPUs, memory, and FPGAs—became an open interconnect standard in 2019. It addresses memory capacity expansion and coherent data sharing between AI accelerators, making it an essential tool for memory disaggregation.

In fact, CXL memory pooling, which addresses memory stranding by allowing unused capacity on one server to serve workloads on another, is already reducing hyperscaler total cost of ownership for memory-intensive workloads. That’s why it’s widely seen as a solution to the “memory wall” problem now confronting AI data centers.

The CXL 4.0 specification, released on Nov. 18, 2025, introduces bundled ports that allow hosts and devices to aggregate multiple physical ports into single logical attachment. It doubles bandwidth to 128 GT/s with PCIe 7.0 integration and fundamentally changes how AI infrastructure architects design memory systems.

Marvell could combine its CXL memory-expansion controllers with XConn CXL switches to broaden its CXL portfolio to support demanding AI workloads. Moreover, XConn’s hybrid switching solution, Apollo 2, could bring new revenue streams for the Santa Clara, Calif.-based connectivity silicon specialist.

XConn’s UALink credentials

At a time when data centers are evolving from single-rack deployments to larger, multi-rack configurations, CXL memory pooling is one crucial connectivity technology to watch, alongside Ultra Accelerator Link (UALink). UALink, also an open connectivity standard, builds on decades of PCIe ecosystem to incorporate high-speed I/O that meets bandwidth, latency, and reach requirements of next-generation AI accelerators.

In fact, UALink combines elements from PCI-Express, AMD’s Infinity Fabric, and modified Ethernet SerDes to create a specialized interconnect for accelerator memory fabrics. It also accomplishes the same raw speed as Ethernet with the latency of PCIe switches, according to the UA Link Consortium, which released UALink 1.0 on April 8, 2025.

UALink optimizes xPU-to-xPU memory communication across accelerators, either directly connected or through a fully connected switch. (Source: UALink Consortium)

With this acquisition, Marvell’s UALink scale-up switch engineering team will get a boost from XConn Technologies’ deep domain expertise in PCIe switching. Eventually, Marvell could bolster its UALink interconnect portfolio by incorporating XConn’s PCIe silicon IP and challenge Nvidia’s NVLink dominance.

This open standard for GPU/accelerator interconnects challenges Nvidia’s NVLink technology, which remains proprietary and is tightly coupled to Nvidia’s hardware. Nvidia’s NVLink, like the UALink open standard, utilizes Ethernet-style SerDes and provides direct, point-to-point connections optimized for accelerator-to-accelerator data exchanges.

However, companies deploying non-Nvidia accelerators or seeking multi-vendor flexibility will inevitably favor UALink technology. At the same time, given Nvidia’s dominance in AI hardware, it’s likely that both UALink and NVLink technologies will coexist in the AI connectivity realm in the coming years.

Marvell’s AI connectivity imperative

The timing of this acquisition is important from two standpoints. First, AI data centers are confronting memory bottlenecks that limit the performance of AI accelerator clusters, and at this technology crossroads, traditional interconnects can’t keep pace with the demands of models with more than 100 billion parameters. Second, moving data between accelerators at sufficient speed requires interconnects capable of terabytes-per-second throughput.

In this backdrop, Marvell’s acquisition of networking chip developer XConn appears to be a strategic step to address the above interconnect imperatives. Marvell CEO Matt Murphy acknowledges that the XConn Technologies deal is part of his company’s connectivity strategy for next-generation AI and cloud data centers.

The cash-and-stock deal is expected to be finalized in early 2026.

See also:

Xconn Shows Off First CXL Switch

CXL Adds Port Bundling to Quench AI Thirst

Open Standard Aims to Connect AI Accelerators

From GPUs to Memory Pools: Why AI Needs Compute Express Link (CXL)

data.title: Mythic Rises from the Ashes with $125 Million Funding Round
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Mythic, the analog compute chip startup, has raised a $125 million in a round led by DCVC, as well as includes strategic investors Honda Motors and Lockheed Martin.

Mythic was founded in 2012 to commercialize analog compute-in-memory technology. The startup’s technology is based on an array of Flash transistors driven at 256 sub-threshold levels for 8-bit compute. Part of the company’s secret sauce is in the significant calibration and compensation schemes required to do this reliably under different environmental conditions. Analog compute is appealing since it can offer very low-power, fast computation for matrix multiplication in AI inference at the edge.

The company underwent a radical restructure and downsizing at the end of 2022 after failing to raise capital. Investors put in $13 million to keep the lights on in March 2023 and appointed co-founder and former CTO Dave Fick as CEO.

Mythic released two first-generation chips, the M1108 (35 TOPS INT8 performance, 4W, launched 2020) and the M1076 (25 TOPS at INT8, 3W, launched 2021). Both chips were designed for video analytics in applications like drones, video surveillance, and industrial machine vision.

Taner Ozcelik, a veteran of Nvidia and OnSemiconductor, joined Mythic as CEO in mid-2024.

The previous incarnation of Mythic leadership had made some mistakes, but under new leadership, the company has rebuilt its architecture, roadmap, software, and strategy, Ozcelik told EE Times.

“Edge is quite nebulous, it’s hard to understand what it is and how to make money in edge,” he said. “But the company also made a lot of rookie mistakes and wasted a lot of money.”

Ozcelik identified 17 issues with Mythic’s approach and technology and detailed them in a six-page memo to the board before taking the job, he said, noting that all the issues were nevertheless fixable.

Part of the issue was that the implementation of Mythic’s first-generation architecture prevented the chips from achieving the performance analog compute is capable of, Ozcelik said. For example, 80 on-chip RISC-V processors were supposed to have utilization below 2%, but they ended up being close to 100% utilized, he said. This was terrible for energy consumption and it overloaded the NoC, crippling communication bandwidth. There were no DACs on-chip, Ozcelik said, and the chip’s ADCs weren’t achieving the right resolution.

“We’ve fixed this in the last year and a half, which has turbocharged the company,” he said.

The fledgling company also taped out its first chip before sufficiently developing its software stack, without taking advantage of technologies like cycle-accurate simulation that are more typically used for hardware-software co-development, Ozcelik said.

“Software is everything,” he said. “The earlier team taped out the chip with no software…that’s a crime.”

Mythic’s SDK can run any model from PyTorch, TensorFlow, or ONNX, and its compiler has come along leaps and bounds, achieving a multiple orders of magnitude performance boost on some workloads, according to Ozcelik.

Chiplet strategy

Mythic’s next-gen chips will be chiplet-based, with configurations ranging from a single 120-TOPS (INT8) chiplet using less than 1 W for vision processing in edge devices, ranging to four and 16 chiplets for bigger edge applications. These products will target robotics, automotive, and defense applications.

The architecture will support 8-bit and 4-bit computation for up to 30 million parameters on chiplet. There is also a DSP on chip that can be used to offload any unsupported operators. Like the first generation, Mythic’s architecture uses a Flash-based analog compute die and a digital control die, which will be 3D-stacked on each other. This vertical stacking is enabled by the technology’s minimal power and cooling requirements, Ozcelik said.

The company is also planning an enormous 1,024-chiplet design on a single substrate to target data center LLM inference. The final Mythic rack-scale server system will take on Nvidia in terms of TCO and power consumption. For 70B models, Ozcelik told EE Times Mythic’s air-cooled servers will achieve two orders of magnitude better TCO per token compared to Nvidia Blackwell-generation hardware. For 1T models, he said the difference could be two orders of magnitude in terms of tokens per second per watt.

Future Mythic generations will also have NAND-Flash-based arrays (as well as the existing NOR version), which means the company will be able to pack bigger models onto a chiplet the same physical size and at lower cost. This will be part of its strategy for bringing bigger LLMs to edge devices, especially in defense, Ozcelik said. As an example, one of Mythic’s defense customers wanted to use an LLM as a natural language interface to the device’s manual, without the need for an internet connection.

Sensor edge

A separate product family, dubbed “Starlight,” will use a Mythic compute chiplet hybrid-bonded under a vision sensor’s photodiode array. The two dies will use less than 1 W between them.

Ozcelik said he noticed a gap in the market for this type of device while previously working at OnSemiconductor.

“One of the biggest challenges for image sensors is low light performance,” he said. “Dynamic range is another major problem, especially in mission critical applications.”

A Mythic AI accelerator could run a neural network to improve low-light performance and dynamic range directly next to the sensor. Image sensors made for applications like cellphones are very small (one-third of an inch), and performance suffers as they get smaller, Ozcelik said. Mythic has a unique opportunity here as its technology is compact, and crucially, it uses very little power, according to Ozcelik (photodiode arrays are extremely thermally sensitive, meaning even a small DSP couldn’t be placed directly under the photodiode array).

Mythic is going to build this sensor and AI accelerator combination itself, and both the accelerator chiplet and the image sensor product will tape out this year, Ozcelik said.

Overall, Ozcelik is pragmatic about the scale of the challenges ahead, particularly given the company’s move into the data center where it will compete with Nvidia.

“[Our advantage] has to be incredibly material,” he said. “It has to be at least one hundred times, hopefully more.”

data.title: Telcos stalling on 5G-SA waiting for 6G
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Five years after the fifth generation of cellular network technology (5G) began rolling out worldwide, the telecom industry faces a major strategic challenge. The first phase quickly expanded coverage by connecting 5G radios to existing 4G LTE networks, known as 5G Non-Standalone (NSA). However, moving to ‘true’ 5G with a cloud-based Standalone (SA) core has faced serious obstacles.

5G was once expected to be a game-changer, but now many Communications Service Providers (CSPs) see it as a costly upgrade with uncertain benefits. As time goes on, 5G SA risks becoming a “silent generation,” used more for behind-the-scenes improvements than for delivering the big changes that were promised.

Architecture of the consumer paradox

The biggest obstacle to rolling out 5G-SA widely is a simple lack of market demand, especially in the consumer market. In the past, each new mobile generation brought new uses: 3G made the mobile web possible, and 4G enabled video streaming. But in its first five years, 5G has struggled to offer a must-have application.

Most smartphone users do not notice key SA features, such as ultra-low latency or support for many connected devices. Research shows that people care more about reliability and value than about whether their phone uses SA or NSA.

Because of this lack of interest, companies have pulled back from charging extra for 5G. In the U.K., British Telecom first offered 5G-SA as a premium service, available only on select high-end handsets, with monthly plans costing more than twice the average revenue per user (ARPU). But since few people signed up, they changed course and included SA features in standard plans at no extra cost.

In the U.S., AT&T finished rolling out 5G-SA nationwide in late 2025, but mobile service revenues grew by only 3.4%, which was just above inflation. This “value perception gap” shows that even when the technology is available, it does not lead to a big increase in consumer spending.

Arrival of 6G and the economic case for waiting

As CSPs consider the high costs of overhauling their core networks, a new factor has come up: the upcoming standardization of 6G. With 6G expected around 2030, its arrival is making companies hesitant to invest more in 5G. Many operators do not want to spend billions on 5G-SA systems that might have to be replaced to meet the new AI and sensing needs of 6G.

This has led to the “odd generation” theory, which suggests that 5G might be a transitional technology like 3G—important technically but less successful commercially than the next generation. Some operators are choosing to get the most out of their current 5G-NSA systems and plan to move straight to 6G-ready cores in the late 2020s.

The pause in upgrades shows up in global spending trends; according to Dell’Oro Group, telecom CapEx either stayed the same or declined in 2025 as operators focused on cash flow and debt reduction rather than chasing the latest technology.

“Capex is past the peak, but it is not falling off a cliff,” said Stefan Pongratz, VP of RAN and Telecom Capex research at Dell’Oro Group. “Still, there will be room to improve capital intensity ratios as operators are reallocating away from blanket coverage builds and toward capacity, quality, automation, and energy performance.”

Fragmented global landscape, regulatory differences

In many countries, the rollout of 5G-SA is now shaped more by government policy than by market demand, leading to regional differences. China is a global outlier, having built over 4.8 million base stations as part of its national industrial policy. The Chinese government sees 5G-SA as key national infrastructure for the “low-altitude economy” and smart manufacturing, prioritizing long-term digital growth over short-term profits for operators.

In contrast, Europe has become a “regulatory quagmire” where SA rollout is slowed by market fragmentation and the high costs of removing equipment from vendors like Huawei, as required by government rules.

The Middle East, especially the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries, has used sovereign wealth to build top-tier networks linked to national “Vision” projects, such as Saudi Vision 2030. And, in Latin America, Brazil has become a leader by requiring 5G-SA deployment in major cities, accelerating the transition beyond what market demand alone would drive.

Regional 5G-SA status and strategic drivers

Infrastructure vendors pivot to programmable networks

As spending on traditional radio access networks (RAN) slows down, infrastructure vendors are changing their strategies to make the SA upgrade more appealing. Ericsson is focusing on “Programmable Networks,” opening network APIs to developers to create new revenue streams.

Nokia is focusing more on the enterprise market with its “Core Engineered Systems” built for cloud-native environments. Last year’s investment from Nvidia focuses more on creating a true cloud and AI network, readying for 6G. At the same time, Samsung is promoting virtualized RAN (vRAN) as a flexible, 6G-ready alternative to traditional hardware.

Huawei, which is absent from several Western markets, is promoting a “5.5G” or 5G-Advanced story, presenting it as a new phase of industrial digitalization. This move to 5G-Advanced (3GPP Releases 18 and 19) introduces AI integration and enhanced support for Extended Reality (XR).

However, these features require a 5G-SA core, creating a “chicken and egg” problem. Operators want the advanced features but are hesitant to pay for the core network upgrades needed to support them.

Regulatory barriers for 5G-SA monetization

The business case for 5G-SA depends heavily on network slicing, which enables operators to split a single physical network into virtual “slices” with guaranteed performance. While this is promising for B2B applications such as industrial robotics and emergency services, the consumer market is still constrained by regulations.

In Europe, strict net neutrality rules make it hard to charge extra for faster “express lanes” for things like high-end gaming or video streaming. These rules set the expectation that a basic connection should work for all apps, making it tough for operators to turn 5G into something more than a standard feature. However, the rules have exceptions for critical services such as healthcare and utilities.

As a result, network slicing is mostly in use in small B2B pilot projects, which account for only a small share of total network traffic. Without a way to make money from the consumer slice, there is little financial reason to move to SA on a large scale right now.

Accidental success story of FWA

The one clear commercial success of the 5G era has been Fixed Wireless Access (FWA), especially in North America. However, FWA’s success actually supports the idea that “good enough” connectivity is more important to users than having the latest technology.

FWA has done well not because it outperforms fiber, but because it offers good enough performance at a better price, especially in sparse population areas, where deploying fiber is costly.

AT&T is offering FWA in a significant way via its 5G network. The company launched its 5G-powered Internet Air FWA product in August 2023 and has seen significant customer growth since.

While FWA uses 5G network capacity, most users do not care whether it is SA or NSA as long as their streaming video works without buffering.

Long road to 6G

Right now, the global rollout of 5G-SA shows uneven progress and lower expectations. While there are real engineering benefits, such as better energy efficiency and higher connection density, they have not yet matched what most consumers want. Operators are making practical choices by delaying expensive upgrades and focusing on getting the most out of current assets and proven revenue sources, such as FWA.

Unless many strong enterprise users emerge soon, 5G-SA may stay in the background as the industry moves on to 6G, serving as a warning for future technology rollouts.
